title: Magnets at Home
intro_hook: Why do magnets stick to some things but not to wood, plastic, or paper?
big_idea: Magnets attract certain materials such as iron and steel. Testing different objects teaches observation, sorting, and material classification.
story: A fridge magnet sticks to a steel door but not to a plastic bottle. The magnet is the same, but the material is different.

cause_process_result.process: Material tested â†’ Attraction or no attraction â†’ Objects sorted

whiteboard.title: Magnets at Home Whiteboard
whiteboard.goal: Understand from zero why a magnet sticks to some objects and not others.
basics: A magnet can pull some objects without touching them. That pull is called magnetic force.
The magnet is not checking the shape or color of the object. It depends mainly on the material.
Iron, steel, nickel, and cobalt are commonly magnetic. Wood, plastic, paper, rubber, copper, and aluminium usually are not attracted by a simple fridge magnet.
A fair test means using the same magnet and testing one object at a time.
whiteboard.formula: Magnet near object -> material checked -> attraction or no attraction -> sorted group
whiteboard.formula_reading: Read it as a process, not a math formula: bring the magnet near, observe the result, then sort the object by material behavior.

symbols.means: what the object is made of
the magnet pulls and sticks or moves the object
symbols.example: steel paper clip moves toward magnet

symbols.means: the object does not respond clearly to the magnet

steps.label: Step 1: Same magnet
steps.detail: Use one magnet for all tests so the magnet strength is not changing.

steps.label: Step 2: One object
steps.detail: Bring the magnet near one safe object, like a paper clip, pencil, spoon, eraser, or bottle cap.

steps.label: Step 3: Observe
steps.detail: Check whether the object moves, sticks, or shows no clear pull.

steps.label: Step 4: Name material
steps.detail: Ask what the object is made of. Steel and iron-containing objects are usually attracted.

steps.label: Step 5: Sort
steps.detail: Make two groups: attracted and not attracted. The pattern tells you which materials are magnetic.

steps.label: Step 6: Explain
steps.detail: A magnet sticks to some things because their material responds to magnetic force; many everyday materials do not.

whiteboard.feedback_prompt: Pause check: are Step 1 and Step 2 clear? If not, tell me whether magnet, material, or fair test is confusing.
whiteboard.worked_example: A steel paper clip sticks, but a wooden pencil does not. The difference is not size or color; it is the material. Steel contains iron, so it responds to the magnet.

common_confusions.fix: Many metals, like copper and aluminium, are not strongly attracted by a normal magnet.
Material matters first. A big plastic ruler will not stick, but a tiny steel pin can.
The result follows a pattern based on material properties.

indian_analogies: Home + School mein yeh dekhte hain: Magnets at Home.

misconceptions.belief: This topic belongs to only one subject.
misconceptions.why_common: A common misconception is that magnets attract all metals. Many metals, such as aluminium or copper, are not strongly attracted to ordinary magnets.
misconceptions.probe: Agar sirf ek subject se explain karein toh kya miss ho jaata hai?
misconceptions.repair: Magnets at Home mein Physics, Math, Chemistry sab ek saath kaam karte hain.

misconceptions.belief: The first visible thing IS the reason, not the start of a chain.
misconceptions.why_common: Students observe the output and skip the mechanism.
misconceptions.probe: Why do magnets stick to some things but not to wood, plastic, or paper?
misconceptions.repair: Cause starts the chain. Result is the end. The process in between is where the science lives.

question_flow.q: Why do magnets stick to some things but not to wood, plastic, or paper?
question_flow.a: Magnets stick to kuch cheezein jaise iron, steel aur nickel kyunki unmein magnetic properties hote hain, lekin ve wood, plastic ya paper se nahi chipte kyunki unmein aisi properties nahi hoti. Yeh magnets ke properties ke kaaran hota hai.

Distance matter karta hai kyunki magnet ki chumbakiya shakti door jaane par kam ho jati hai, isliye magnet ke prabhav ko mahsoos karne ke liye hume use kareeb rakhna padta hai. Jab hum magnet ko kisi cheez ke paas le jaate hain to uski chumbakiya shakti zyada mahsoos hoti hai. Isliye distance magnet ki ch
Nahin, sab metals magnetic nahi hote, jaise ki copper, aluminium aur tin magnetic nahi hote, lekin iron, nickel aur cobalt magnetic hote hain. Yeh metals ke properties par nirbhar karta hai.

question_flow.q: Where are magnets hidden in machines?
question_flow.a: Magnets ghar ke machines mein chhupi hui hoti hain, jaise ki refrigerator, TV, aur computer mein. In machines mein magnets ko specific parts jaise ki door, screen, aur speaker mein use kiya jata hai. Isse machines ka proper function hota hai aur humein convenience milti hai.

question_flow.q: In this flow â€” Magnet brought near object -> Material tested -> Attraction or no attraction â€” what exactly happens at each step?
question_flow.a: Sabse pehle, magnet ko object ke paas laya jata hai, phir us material ka test kiya jata hai, aur phir dekha jata hai ki magnet us object ko attract karta hai ya nahi, isse humein pata chalta hai ki woh material chumbakiya hai ya nahi. Is prakriya mein, magnet ka chumbakiya kshetra object par kaam karta hai. Agar object

question_flow.q: Ab tu mujhe bata â€” apne words mein. Ek 5 saal ke bachhe ko kaise samjhayega?
question_flow.a: Magnet ek aisa chij hai jo cheezon ko attract karta hai, jaise ki fridge par hum magnet lagate hain aur wo chipka rehta hai. Hum isse apne ghar mein fridge, almirah aur dusre jagah par use karte hain. Isse hum cheezon ko sahi jagah par rakh sakte hain aur wo khoye nahi.

question_flow.q: Which object is most likely to be attracted by a magnet?
question_flow.a: Ek chhota sa iron ka screw ya nail magnet ke dwara akarshit hone ki sabse adhik sambhavana hai kyunki iron magnet ko attract karta hai. Isliye, magnet iron objects ko attract karega.

practice_problems.q: Which object is most likely to be attracted by a magnet?
practice_problems.hint: Steel contains iron, so a steel paper clip is usually attracted by a magnet.
practice_problems.answer: Steel contains iron, so a steel paper clip is usually attracted by a magnet.

practice_problems.q: What would happen if distance from object becomes very low?
practice_problems.answer: Consider how changing this variable shifts each step of the process.

practice_problems.q: What would happen if distance from object becomes very high?
practice_problems.answer: Consider how changing this variable shifts each step of the process.

practice_problems.q: What would change if the place, material, time, or weather changes?
practice_problems.answer: Consider how changing this variable shifts each step of the process.

teaching_styles.simple: Magnets at Home shows that daily life has a science reason. Notice what changes first, then describe the result in simple words.
teaching_styles.standard: In Magnets at Home, one or more factors such as distance from object change what we observe. The idea connects Physics, Math, Chemistry and helps explain the situation beyond memorizing a fact.
teaching_styles.deep: A deeper explanation of Magnets at Home connects variables, evidence, and cause-effect. Students should identify the changing factor, keep other conditions similar, compare observations, and explain the result using the relevant subject lenses. Magnets attract certain materials such as iron and steel. Testing different objects teaches observation, sorting, and material classification.
teaching_styles.hinglish: Dekh yaar â€” Magnets at Home mein kya ho raha hai? Observe karo pehle, phir samjhenge.
teaching_styles.story: A fridge magnet sticks to a steel door but not to a plastic bottle. The magnet is the same, but the material is different.

learner_adaptations.class_1_3: Notice the situation: Why do magnets stick to some things but not to wood, plastic, or paper. Say what you can see, hear, feel, or compare safely.
learner_adaptations.class_4_5: Name the main factor and compare two cases. Example: less vs more distance from object.
learner_adaptations.class_6_8: Explain the cause-effect chain: Magnet brought near object â†’ Material tested â†’ Attraction or no attraction â†’ Objects sorted.
learner_adaptations.class_9_10: Use variables, controls, evidence, trade-offs, and subject links to justify a better real-life decision.
learner_adaptations.struggling: Start with the hook only. Ask what they observe. Don't explain yet.
learner_adaptations.fast_learner: Push to deep mode. Connect all subjects: Physics, Math, Chemistry.

subject_lenses.Physics: Magnetic force can pull some materials without touching them.
subject_lenses.Math: Count how many tested objects are magnetic and make groups.
subject_lenses.Chemistry: Different materials have different composition; iron-containing materials often respond.

wonder_tangents.hinglish: By the way â€” Magnets at Home ka concept aur jagah bhi kaam karta hai: Fridge magnets, Compass basics, School boards.
wonder_tangents.career_connection: Electrical engineering, recycling, motors, navigation, product design.

daily_wonders: Aaj magnets at home se related kuch notice karo.
Why do magnets stick to some things but not to wood, plastic, or paper?
What would happen if distance from object becomes very low?

indian_legacy.connection: Aryabhata, a renowned Indian mathematician and astronomer, discussed the concept of magnetism in his work Aryabhatiya. His understanding of the Earth's magnetic properties laid the foundation for further research in the field, which can be connected to the concept of magnets attracting certain materials, allowing students to explore and understand the properties of magnets at home.
indian_legacy.story_hook: Imagine you are a young apprentice to Aryabhata, tasked with discovering which objects in the village are attracted to a mysterious stone that sticks to iron tools. As you test different objects, you begin to unravel the secrets of magnetism and its role in the world around you.

home_investigation.title: Ghar pe Try Karo: Magnets at Home
materials: Ghar mein available items (see experiment below)
steps: Test a magnet on safe household objects with adult supervision. Do not use magnets near phones, cards, watches, or medical devices.
home_investigation.safety_note: Adult supervision recommended for heat, electricity, or chemicals.
home_investigation.share_with_sakha: Kya hua? Kya unexpected tha? Apne words mein batao.

life_decision: Test safely, sort materials, and avoid using magnets near sensitive devices.
career_connections: Electrical engineering, recycling, motors, navigation, product design.
why_matters: It helps young students see that material properties decide how objects behave.
teach_back_prompt: Explain to a younger student why a magnet sticks to a steel paper clip but not to paper or plastic.

learning_showcase.try_first: Before Sakha explains, predict three objects: one that will stick, one that will not, and one you are unsure about.
learning_showcase.simple_idea: A magnet pulls some materials. The material matters more than color, shape, or size.
learning_showcase.formal_idea: Magnetic force attracts some iron-containing materials such as iron and steel, while many other materials show no clear attraction.
learning_showcase.trap_fix: Steel usually sticks, but copper, aluminium, paper, wood, and plastic usually do not stick to a simple magnet.
learning_showcase.memory_anchor: Think of a magnet as a material tester: same magnet, different materials, different results.
learning_showcase.friend_nudge: If this feels confusing, name the object you are imagining and Sakha will sort it with you.
learning_showcase.teach_back_goal: Explain why a steel paper clip sticks but paper and plastic do not.
review_plan: In 3 days: explain why all metals are not magnetic
